title: Exhibit C — tier fallback sequencing and billing exceptions
preview:
From: Julie Smith <julie.smith@olivehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: procurement@olivehealth.com
Date: Fri, 22 May 2026 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Questions on Exhibit C fallback sequencing

Ben —

Thanks for sending the updated pricing schedule. Our procurement team reviewed Exhibit C and we have a few open items before legal can finalise the SOW. High level: the fallback sequencing (how traffic moves from primary model variants to the backup tiers) is functionally fine, but the contract language leaves operational ambiguity on billing and credits when a fallback occurs at high frequency.

Specific asks:
- Confirm whether the billing true-up for any tokens processed by the fallback tier is monthly or quarterly. Our finance team needs certainty for forecasting.
- The clause describing the "floor protection" appears to permit retroactive true-up but doesn't state an upper bound on credits when fallback is used by >20% of requests in a billing period. We need clarity/cap.
- Please provide a short version of the mutual action plan showing escalation steps and who toggles fallback policy (customer, Redwood, or automated).

Attached: OLIVE_SOW_v4.docx (annotated by Procurement).

Can you loop in legal and ops on the Redwood side — and propose wording we can review?

Appreciate the quick turnaround — we are targeting sign-off by next week.

Julie
Julie Smith | Sr. Counsel, Procurement
Olive Health
---
Attachment: OLIVE_SOW_v4.docx


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Julie Smith <julie.smith@olivehealth.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 22 May 2026 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Questions on Exhibit C fallback sequencing

Julie —

Thanks for the clear notes and the annotated SOW. Quick response with a proposed path forward so we can keep the sign-off cadence:

Proposed mutual action plan (high level):
1) Redwood to propose contract language for Exhibit C fallback (we attach an inline markup). Due: today EOD.
2) Olive to confirm whether monthly vs quarterly true-up is acceptable or provide an alternative. Due: Tue 5/26.
3) Ops alignment call (30 minutes) to walk through routing and alerts; attendees: Redwood ops (Karthik), Olive platform leads. Proposed slot 5/27.
4) Final markup for DocuSign and SOW consolidation. Target: 5/28.

On the billing specifics: we typically perform billing true-ups on a monthly cadence for hosted customers with usage-based pricing. For dedicated/reserved deployments we use quarterly reconciliations by default. That said, we're willing to put the cadence explicitly in Exhibit C and allow Olive to pick monthly or quarterly in the order form.

Suggested contract language (draft snippet for Exhibit C fallback):
"If Redwood’s routing policy transitions a portion of Customer traffic to a fallback tier, usage will be billed at the rates specified for the fallback tier. Any billing true-up resulting from fallback activity will occur on the cadence selected in the Order Form (monthly or quarterly). For the first 90 days following Go-Live, if fallback activity exceeds 20% of total requests in any billing period, Redwood will apply a one-time operational credit equal to 10% of charges attributable to the fallback tier for that billing period, subject to a maximum aggregate credit of $50,000. After the initial 90-day period, fallback activity will be billed without the one-time credit unless otherwise agreed in writing."

I've attached a marked-up Exhibit_C (Exhibit_C_markup_v3.pdf) with inline notes on clause numbering and a suggested cap. Karthik, can you confirm we can operationalize the 20% monitor and the 10% credit automation?

Happy to hop on a quick call.

Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | +1 415-555-0192
---
Attachment: Exhibit_C_markup_v3.pdf


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
To: Julie Smith <julie.smith@olivehealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, 23 May 2026 08:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Questions on Exhibit C fallback sequencing — legal redline and operational note

Julie / Ben —

Thanks — I reviewed Ben's suggested snippet and Olive's annotated SOW. A few legal redline items and implementation asks below. I copied Karthik for ops confirmation.

Redline / comments (Exhibit C):
- Clause ref: 4.2.3 (Fallback billing). Replace "billing true-up resulting from fallback activity will occur on the cadence selected in the Order Form" with "billing true-up for fallback activity shall occur monthly unless the Order Form expressly elects quarterly reconciliation." Keeps default monthly for predictability.
- Cap on credits: we can agree a one-time operational credit for onboarding protection; however, rather than a hard $50,000 cap, we prefer a percentage-based cap tied to the first quarter's committed spend (e.g., maximum credit equal to 15% of committed spend for Q1). That aligns incentives.
- Measurement: define "fallback activity" as "% of successful inference requests where routing policy routes to fallback tier, measured at the request-response level (excluding automated health-check traffic)."
- Audit & evidence: Olive can request a breakdown and Redwood will provide logs of routing decisions for the disputed billing period. Add a 30-day window for disputes.

Operational ask for Karthik: can Redwood's billing pipeline emit a monthly fallback metric and a CSV export with per-route counts so Olive can reconcile? If yes, we'll include that as an operational exhibit to the SOW.

Edits attached as tracked changes in Exhibit_C_markup_v3.pdf. Ben — if Olive accepts the monthly default and the measurement clause, we can update the Order Form to elect Q1 committed-spend based cap language if Olive prefers that model.

Lauren Bishop
Director, Commercial Contracts | Redwood Inference
lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com


From: Julie Smith <julie.smith@olivehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 25 May 2026 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Questions on Exhibit C fallback sequencing — Olive response

Ben / Lauren —

Thank you for the quick turnaround and the concrete language. Olive is comfortable with the proposed measurement definition and the 30-day dispute window. A few points we need to lock down before I can take the SOW to my CFO:

1) Billing cadence: we accept monthly as the default. We will elect monthly on the Order Form.
2) Credit cap: Olive prefers a simple, predictable cap. The commitment-based cap sounds reasonable; please insert the following line to Exhibit C: "Maximum onboarding credit shall be 15% of Customer's committed spend for the first full calendar quarter following Go-Live." We'll need the committed spend number in the Order Form.
3) Fast-exceed clause: if fallback exceeds 50% for any month in the first quarter, Olive requests an additional operational credit equal to 5% of that month's fallback charges (this is not intended to be punitive — it's to cover our remediation/headcount costs).

If Redwood accepts #3 we are happy to proceed to DocuSign. I've attached a short note and a DocuSign link for the SOW consolidation: https://docusign.com/OLV123456 (please confirm if you prefer to receive the signed SOW route through your legal contact).

On the Ops side: Karthik, the CSV export sounds good. Do you also expose per-model latency buckets in that export? That will help us correlate fallback events with observed latency spikes.

Appreciate it — we are aiming to get legal sign-off by EOD Wednesday.

Julie
Julie Smith | Sr. Counsel, Procurement
Olive Health

- Clause ref: 4.2.3 (Fallback billing). Replace "billing true-up resulting from fallback activity will occur on the cadence selected in the Order Form" with "billing true-up for fallback activity shall occur monthly unless the Order Form expressly elects quarterly reconciliation." Keeps default monthly for predictability.
- Cap on credits: we can agree a one-time operational credit for onboarding protection; however, rather than a hard $50,000 cap, we prefer a percentage-based cap tied to the first quarter's committed spend (e.g., maximum credit equal to 15% of committed spend for Q1). That aligns incentives.
- Measurement: define "fallback activity" as "% of successful inference requests where routing policy routes to fallback tier, measured at the request-response level (excluding automated health-check traffic)."
- Audit & evidence: Olive can request a breakdown and Redwood will provide logs of routing decisions for the disputed billing period. Add a 30-day window for disputes.

Operational ask for Karthik: can Redwood's billing pipeline emit a monthly fallback metric and a CSV export with per-route counts so Olive can reconcile? If yes, we'll include that as an operational exhibit to the SOW.

Edits attached as tracked changes in Exhibit_C_markup_v3.pdf. Ben — if Olive accepts the monthly default and the measurement clause, we can update the Order Form to elect Q1 committed-spend based cap language if Olive prefers that model.

Lauren Bishop
Director, Commercial Contracts | Redwood Inference
lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com

Subject: Re: Questions on Exhibit C fallback sequencing — Olive response

Ben / Lauren —

Thank you for the quick turnaround and the concrete language. Olive is comfortable with the proposed measurement definition and the 30-day dispute window. A few points we need to lock down before I can take the SOW to my CFO:

1) Billing cadence: we accept monthly as the default. We will elect monthly on the Order Form.
2) Credit cap: Olive prefers a simple, predictable cap. The commitment-based cap sounds reasonable; please insert the following line to Exhibit C: "Maximum onboarding credit shall be 15% of Customer's committed spend for the first full calendar quarter following Go-Live." We'll need the committed spend number in the Order Form.
3) Fast-exceed clause: if fallback exceeds 50% for any month in the first quarter, Olive requests an additional operational credit equal to 5% of that month's fallback charges (this is not intended to be punitive — it's to cover our remediation/headcount costs).

If Redwood accepts #3 we are happy to proceed to DocuSign. I've attached a short note and a DocuSign link for the SOW consolidation: https://docusign.com/OLV123456 (please confirm if you prefer to receive the signed SOW route through your legal contact).

title: Mutual action plan: POC environment, API keys & peering timeline
Retain Brightleaf — concession approval request
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 18 May 2026 16:12:00 -0700
Subject: Retain Brightleaf — concession approval request

Team —

Quick summary: Brightleaf's renewal is due 2026-06-15 (current ARR $1.2M). They told us last week they are evaluating two competitors and have pushed back on list pricing. They want a 30% price reduction and a term reset to start a new 12-month term now. They also raised the Q1 incident (brief outage) as reason to ask for an SLA credit in the contract.

Customer asks (verbatim):
- 30% discount for renewal year
- Reset term to 12 months starting on signing
- One-time service credit equivalent to 30 days SLA credit for Q1 outage

What I recommend (preserving gross margin while keeping them):
- Approve a single-year concession: 18% discount on renewal invoice
- One-time operational credit of $40,000 (applied to first invoice), documented as a service credit in the amendment
- Grant the term reset (12 months) but add a clause that future renewals will be no earlier than 11 months out (to prevent mid-year resets)

Open items I need approval on:
1) Discount level (I recommended 18%)
2) $40k one-time credit (finance will need the credit memo approval)
3) SLA language: Monica, can CS scope the credit language? Legal: Lauren, we need a short clause for the amendment and confirmation this doesn't create ongoing SLA liability beyond the one-time credit.

Attachments: Brightleaf_Renewal_Req.xlsx (overview of ask, churn risk, ARR math), current_contract.pdf (for reference).

Timing: Brightleaf expects a formal written offer by EOD Thursday 2026-05-21. I prefer to get exec sign-off today/tomorrow so we can move fast.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Strategic Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 19 May 2026 09:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Retain Brightleaf — concession approval request

Ben —

Thanks for the clear write-up. A few legal points before we finalize language:
- The one-time credit needs to be explicitly labeled as \"non-recurring service credit\" in the amendment; do not change recurring rates or introduce a permanent discount schedule.
- Term-reset clause: add a sentence that the renewed term will not permit retroactive pro-rating or additional resets within the following 12 months. That protects us from repeated resets.
- SLA: outline a single-line addendum stating \"Customer will receive a one-time service credit of $40,000 in satisfaction of the Q1 incident; no additional SLA remedies are granted.\" If Finance requires a different accounting label, note it in the amendment.

If Finance signs off on the $40k accounting treatment, legal is OK with the approach. Final redline to amendment_draft_redline.pdf.

Lauren
Lauren Bishop
Associate General Counsel
Redwood Inference

- One-time credit: Approve up to $40,000 as a non-recurring credit applied to the first invoice. Finance must confirm accounting and update the internal credit memo.
- Term reset: Approve a 12-month reset effective on signing. Legal to include language preventing further mid-term resets within the first 12 months after execution.
- SLA addendum: Approve a one-time service credit of $40,000 and explicit language that this is the sole remedy for the referenced incident. Monica, please prepare the exact SLA addendum text for the amendment.

Ben, proceed to present this offer to procurement. Get their written acceptance and return a signed amendment; I'll notify Finance to expect the credit memo. If procurement pushes for higher discount, escalate back to me — no further authority without CFO approval.

title: SIG: KV-cache retention & model training commitments
preview:
From: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: MediSecure Procurement <procurement@medisecure.com>
Date: Thu, 20 May 2026 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the SIG answers you provided earlier — our security team has one last set of clarifying questions before we close the questionnaire: specifically about the inference KV cache and retention/usage of request-response logs for model training. Could you confirm:  

- Whether the KV cache (model key/value state used during inference) is ever written to durable storage or logs, and if so what the retention window is.
- The retention window for request/response logs (prompts and model outputs) for your hosted API by default, and whether that can be configured to 0 for managed enterprise deployments.
- Whether customer prompt/response content is ever used to further train or fine-tune models without an explicit opt-in.

We need a short, procurement-ready statement we can attach to our SIG response (one paragraph + any relevant artifacts). Also let us know whether you can provide an exported evidence PDF or SOC 2 control mapping for our records.

Appreciate the quick help — we are on a tight procurement timeline.

Thanks,
Brad
Brad Keller
Senior InfoSec Analyst, MediSecure Health
security@medisecure.com



From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
Date: Fri, 21 May 2026 09:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Hi Brad — thanks for the clear list. Below is a concise, procurement-friendly statement followed by the technical details. I attached a short PDF with the same wording plus a SOC 2 controls summary.

Procurement statement (one-paragraph):
Redwood Inference treats customer request/response content and inference state as customer data. By default, hosted API request/response logs are retained for 30 days to support debugging, abuse detection, and service reliability; customers on Redwood Dedicated or Private deployments may configure log retention to meet contractual requirements, including zero retention. The KV cache used during inference is an in-memory/session state and is not persisted to long-term storage as part of normal operation. Redwood does not use customer prompt or response content to train or fine-tune models without explicit, opt-in written consent. Audit access to customer data is tightly controlled, logged, and governed by our SOC 2 controls.

Technical details / clarifications:
- KV cache:  
  - The KV cache (attention keys/values and ephemeral decoding state) exists in RAM on the serving node for the duration of the session. It may be serialized in memory for intra-cluster transfer during scaling operations but is not written to durable customer-accessible object stores as part of normal inference. Redwood engineers do not persist KV cache to long-term storage except under rare, documented incident response workflows (and those actions are logged and subject to approval).
- Prompt/response logs (hosted):  
  - Default retention: 30 days. Purpose: debugging, abuse detection, quality monitoring.
  - Enterprise/Dedicated/Private: retention is configurable in contract; default for Dedicated can be set to match customer requirements including 0 days (no retention).
- Training on customer data:  
  - Redwood does not use customer prompt/response content to train or update model weights unless the customer provides explicit opt-in via a written agreement. Aggregated telemetry and non-identifiable operational metrics may be used to improve platform reliability but are not traceable to individual prompt content.
- Access controls and logging:  
  - Access to raw request/response data is strictly limited to authorized roles (SRE and Support under break-glass with approvals). All such access is logged and reviewed as part of SOC 2 change and access control processes.

If you need a short signed attestation line for procurement ("Redwood attests that..."), let me know and I can coordinate with Legal to provide a dated statement.

Attached: redwood-sig-answers-20260522.pdf (procurement statement + control mapping), soc2-controls-summary-2026.pdf

Best,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
support@redwood.com



From: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 21 May 2026 14:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question

Aisha — this is great, thank you. Two quick asks before we mark the SIG row as closed:  

1) Procurement wants a single-line attestation we can drop into our vendor register (e.g., "Redwood attests that customer data is not used to train models without explicit consent"). Can Legal sign/date that?
2) Can you confirm whether audit logs that record engineer access are retained for at least 12 months (our control requires 12 months of audit trail).

If you can get me the one-line attestation and confirmation on audit log retention by EOD Monday (May 24) that would keep us on schedule.

Thanks,
Brad

To: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>
Cc: Brad Keller <brad.keller@medisecure.com>

Subject: Fwd: SIG follow-up: KV cache + training question — attestation & audit log retention

Draft language below that I used in the PDF; please review and let me know if Legal is comfortable with the attestation and whether Compliance can confirm the log retention policy.

Draft attestation (suggested):
"Redwood Inference attests that customer prompts, responses, and inference state are not used to train or fine-tune model weights without explicit, written customer consent. Access to customer data is logged and restricted to authorized personnel; audit logs are retained in accordance with Redwood's SOC 2 controls."

Questions: 
- Can we add a parenthetical to explicitly say audit logs are retained for 12 months? (procurement needs that).
- Is the attestation ok to be signed by our Head of Legal / Compliance, or does it need executive sign-off?

Thanks — Aisha

title: Post-call: Deployment economics and next steps for PeakCart
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@peakcart.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 18 May 2026 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: Deployment economics and next steps for PeakCart

Hi Emily / Oliver —

Thanks again for carving out time today. Quick recap from our side and proposed next steps so we’re aligned before the pricing work: 

- Goal: move from PoC -> production for PeakCart search & recommendations (approx. 120M tokens/mo expected after Q1 ramp).
- Primary constraints you shared: strict data residency for EU users (we discussed hybrid VPC), predictable tail latency under 300ms for search queries, and a preference to avoid large upfront infra shutdown risk.

Options we discussed (high-level):
- Hosted API: lowest friction, per-token billing. Good for bursty traffic early in PoC; potential unit-cost premium at scale.
- Dedicated capacity (reserved GPU pools): lower per-token equivalent cost for steady throughput; requires a commit (monthly or annual).
- Private VPC / on-prem: best for data residency & compliance; higher ops overhead and longer lead time.

Action items I’m taking: build a comparator that models your three usage profiles (baseline PoC, Q1 ramp, steady-state) across per-token vs reserved capacity economics and include quantized throughput sensitivity. I’ll share an editable spreadsheet + a short two-slide summary.

Proposed next steps:
1) I’ll send the cost model by EOD Wednesday (we’ll include a break-even chart and recommended commit levels).
2) If the numbers look reasonable, schedule a 30-min follow-up to walk the model and runway (suggest Thu/Fri next week).
3) Marissa will scope the security checklist for a VPC option in parallel.

If I missed anything or you want different traffic scenarios modeled, please ping and I’ll include them.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | (415) 555-0142

---
Attachment note: I’ll attach a first-pass model in the next message (spreadsheet + short brief).

From: Emily Zhang <emily@peakcart.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Oliver Park <oliver@peakcart.com>
Date: Tue, 19 May 2026 09:12:33 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: Deployment economics and next steps for PeakCart

Ben —

Appreciate the recap and plan. Couple of clarifications before you finalize the model: 

1) Traffic mix: ~70% read (search), 30% generate (product description variations). For reads we need <300ms P95; generation can tolerate up to 1s.
2) Data residency: EU user profiles must not leave EU datacenter; US users can go to hosted. That’s why we’re curious about a hybrid approach (hosted + VPC for EU).
3) Peak concurrency: during promotions we can spike to ~1,200 qps sustained for 15–30 minutes, otherwise baseline 120 qps.

Two asks: please include a hybrid routing scenario (hosted for US, dedicated or private for EU) and show the incremental cost to keep EU-resident data fully on VPC. Also show committed vs metered examples (monthly commit at 3m/6m/12m cadence).

Happy to take the follow-up call after we see the spreadsheet.

Thanks,
Emily Zhang
Head of Platform, PeakCart
emily@peakcart.com

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Emily Zhang <emily@peakcart.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Oliver Park <oliver@peakcart.com>
Date: Wed, 20 May 2026 11:08:12 -0700
Subject: RE: Post-call: Deployment economics and next steps for PeakCart

Emily — attaching the model and a short brief as promised. High-level notes first, then a suggested commit recommendation.

Attachments: PeakCart_Redwood_Cost_Model.xlsx, Redwood_deploy-options-brief.pdf

High-level takeaways from the model (attached):
- For baseline PoC (120 qps / ~120M tokens/month), hosted per-token is simplest and likely lowest TCO in month 1–3 because you avoid commit overhead.
- At steady-state (projected 120M tokens/mo with predictable traffic), a dedicated capacity commit at ~70% utilization shows ~35–45% lower effective per-token cost vs hosted metered. The spreadsheet includes an assumed hourly GPU price and an efficiency factor for batching/KV cache.
- Hybrid routing (Host US / Dedicated EU): incremental cost to keep EU traffic in a VPC is ~+22% vs fully hosted (includes VPC egress & reserved infra markup). However, hybrid gives you data residency guarantees and predictable latency.
- Private on-prem remains the most expensive in ops uplift and initial SOW (~3–4x first-year TCO vs hosted) but provides the strongest control for regulated use cases.

Suggested commit recommendation (if you plan to commit):
- Start with a 6-month reserved pool that covers 60–70% of expected steady throughput, with overflow to hosted API. This hedges against over-commit early in ramp while capturing material unit-cost savings.

Next steps I propose:
1) Review the attached workbook (editable) and mark any input assumptions you want changed.
2) 30-min walkthrough — I’m available Thu 21st 10–11am PT or Fri 22nd 1–3pm PT. Pick one and I’ll send a calendar invite.
3) Marissa will drop a VPC checklist in the same Drive folder this afternoon.

If helpful, we can also run a short 2-week pilot on Dedicated to validate P95 latency at your typical traffic shape; I noted that as an optional line item in the brief.

Best,
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
